_comment: D159 Phase F bounded strategic logistics/rail network. These values teach the rail asymmetry and feed a capped, opt-in bridge priority. They do not create a campaign map and do not add or author any new playable battle.

US.name: Union rail system
US.repairCapacity: USMRR and Haupt's Construction Corps made rail operation and repair a renewable military asset.
US.teaching: The Union did not merely have more track. It had rolling stock, shops, mechanics, a national army railroad bureau, and the state capacity to turn rails into strategic mobility.
sources: HISTORICAL-DATA-ECONOMY.md railroads dossier, citing Battlefield Trust, Essential Civil War Curriculum, NARA Prologue, and McPherson.
HISTORICAL-DATA-ECONOMY.md macro snapshot, citing 1860 US Census, McPherson, NPS, and EH.net.

CS.name: Confederate rail system
CS.repairCapacity: About 100 separate companies, gauge breaks, worn rail, and near-zero wartime rail-iron production turned local victories into a shrinking supply system.
CS.teaching: The Confederate economy could field dangerous armies, but slavery-backed wealth was not convertible into rail iron, locomotives, repair shops, or food delivery at national-war scale.
sources: HISTORICAL-DATA-ECONOMY.md railroads dossier, citing Battlefield Trust, Essential Civil War Curriculum, Trains Magazine, Black, and Turner.
HISTORICAL-DATA-ECONOMY.md agriculture and macro dossiers, citing Hurt, Gates, Black, 1860 US Census, and McPherson.

E.name: Eastern Theater
E.note: Dense rail corridors around Washington, Richmond, the Valley, and Petersburg made depots decisive but still left armies marching from railhead to field.
sources: HISTORICAL-DATA-ECONOMY.md railroads dossier, citing Battlefield Trust and Trains Magazine.
GRAND-STRATEGY-PLAN.md strategic-map/logistics sections.

W.name: Western Theater
W.note: Long distances, river-rail junctions, and vulnerable corridors made Western armies depend on repair, depots, and rolling-stock throughput.
sources: HISTORICAL-DATA-ECONOMY.md railroads dossier, citing Emerging Civil War and Essential Civil War Curriculum.
HISTORICAL-DATA-ECONOMY.md agriculture dossier, citing Hurt, Black, and Gates.

TM.name: Trans-Mississippi
TM.note: Sparse rails and great distances keep this theater dependent on wagon roads, rivers, local supply, and short operational pulses.
sources: HISTORICAL-DATA-ECONOMY.md railroads dossier, citing Battlefield Trust and ECWC.
GRAND-STRATEGY-PLAN.md theater-map and logistics sections.

N.name: Naval and river operations
N.note: Water transport could bypass weak rail corridors, especially for the Union, but ports and rivers still had to connect to depots ashore.
sources: HISTORICAL-DATA-ECONOMY.md cotton-blockade and railroads dossiers.
GRAND-STRATEGY-PLAN.md logistics, naval, and blockade sections.

benchmarks.label: Union XI and XII Corps transfer to relieve Chattanooga
benchmarks.lesson: The Union could shift corps across multiple states quickly because it combined rail capacity, management, rolling stock, and repair organization.
sources: HISTORICAL-DATA-ECONOMY.md railroads dossier, citing Emerging Civil War.
HISTORICAL-DATA-ECONOMY.md railroads dossier, citing Essential Civil War Curriculum.

benchmarks.label: Longstreet's corps to Chickamauga
benchmarks.lesson: The Confederate transfer was operationally impressive but exposed the cost of transfers, gauge breaks, thin rolling stock, and piecemeal arrival.
sources: HISTORICAL-DATA-ECONOMY.md railroads dossier, citing Emerging Civil War.
HISTORICAL-DATA-ECONOMY.md railroads dossier, citing Essential Civil War Curriculum.

debates.title: Material inevitability vs organization
debates.summary: The South lacked iron, shops, and rolling stock, but political fragmentation and late national coordination made the material problem worse.
sources: HISTORICAL-DATA-ECONOMY.md debate card citing Robert C. Black, The Railroads of the Confederacy.
HISTORICAL-DATA-ECONOMY.md debate card citing George E. Turner and Battlefield Trust.

debates.title: Did railroads decide the war?
debates.summary: Rails did not fight battles by themselves. They shaped concentration, supply, repair, and strategic tempo, which is why this model feeds the existing bridge instead of forcing outcomes.
sources: HISTORICAL-DATA-ECONOMY.md debate card citing James M. McPherson.
HISTORICAL-DATA-ECONOMY.md debate card citing Weber and Clark on USMRR/Haupt management.
